flashcards.back: A memory management technique that creates an illusion of a larger address space than physically available by using disk space as an extension of RAM.
An exception that occurs when a program accesses a memory page that is not currently in physical RAM, causing the OS to load it from disk.
A page loading strategy where pages are loaded into memory only when explicitly requested by a program, not in advance.
A condition where the system spends most of its time swapping pages in and out of memory rather than executing processes, caused by excessive page faults.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model?
flashcards.back: A model that defines the set of pages a process is currently using actively; the OS ensures enough frames are allocated to hold this working set.
A technique where parent and child processes share the same memory pages until one attempts to modify a page, at which point a private copy is created.

flashcards.front: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: A hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations to speed up memory access without consulting the page table.

flashcards.front: Explain the FIFO page replacement algorithm.
flashcards.back: The oldest page in memory is removed first when a new page needs to be loaded. Simple but can suffer from Belady's anomaly.
Least Recently Used algorithm evicts the page that has not been accessed for the longest time. More accurate but requires hardware support.
A phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can paradoxically increase page faults, specifically occurring with FIFO algorithm.

flashcards.front: What is an inverted page table?
flashcards.back: A page table structure that maintains one entry per physical frame rather than per virtual page, reducing memory overhead for large address spaces.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a valid-invalid bit in a page table?
flashcards.back: It indicates whether a page is currently resident in physical memory (valid) or not (invalid), triggering a page fault if accessed when invalid.
The tendency of a processor to access the same set of memory locations repetitively over a short period, forming temporal and spatial locality.
A memory management scheme that eliminates the need for contiguous allocation by dividing physical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames and logical memory into pages.

flashcards.front: What is the page table entry structure?
flashcards.back: Contains frame number, valid/invalid bit, protection bits (read/write/execute), dirty bit (modified), and reference/use bit for replacement decisions.
